# EX09 PROJECT

## 📝 Data Analysis Results

mY analysis focused on the experience gap among students to see if beginners faced a disproportionate challenge. Below are the key findings from our survey data, visualized in three ways.

### 1. The Breakdown of Experience
Before analyzing difficulty, we mapped out the "experience distribution" of our sample. As shown in this chart, most respondents have "None to less than one month!" of experience. This highlights the importance of beginner-focused instruction.

[Chart showing most students are beginners](static/imgs/experience.png)

### 2. The Experience-Difficulty Link
Next, we directly compared experience level against the average difficulty rating (1-7 scale). The bar chart clearly shows that beginners reported an average difficulty of **4.50**, which is significantly higher than the **3.00** difficulty reported by experts. This gap confirms a beginning hump in learning.

[Bar chart showing beginners find the course significantly more difficulty](static/imgs/difficulty.png)

### 3. Understanding vs. Difficulty
Finally, we analyzed whether high perceived difficulty led to a corresponding drop in final understanding. The scatter plot shows a **weak** negative correlation. Many students are still reporting a maximum understanding score (6 or 7) even when difficulty ratings are at their highest. This suggests that while challenging, the curriculum does effectively move students toward mastery.

[Scatter plot showing understanding scores remain high regardless of difficulty](static/imgs/correlation.png)

---

## 📈 Final Recommendations
To bridge the difficulty gap for the beginner majority, we recommend the use of **scaffolded practice problems** in the early units. This initial investment from instructional staff will provide critical support during the steep, initial learning phase and can directly translate into higher understanding scores and a more inclusive learning experience.
